Heritage of Fire

Four centuries of Tosa forging, struck by the fourth-generation hand.

Tosa free-forging (Tosa uchi-hamono) traces back roughly four hundred years, when the forests of Kochi demanded tools that could fell, carve and clear. Toyokuni was founded in 1946 and is worked today by a fourth-generation smith of the Hamaguchi line — drawing steel out on the anvil rather than stamping it, so each blade carries the marks of its own making.

Free-forging means no two pieces are identical. Fire, hammer and water set the steel; the Damascus pattern that rises on the polish is the record of those layers folded under heat.

~400
Years of Tosa forging

Generation smith

The forged surface — 15 layers brought up by polish

Why this blade

Built to do the work of several tools, and to outlast all of them.

Aogami #2 core · 15 forge-welded layers

A kennata is a hybrid — the reach and point of a small sword with the chopping spine of a nata. The featured configuration runs a high-carbon Aogami #2 (Blue-2) core forge-welded between fifteen layers of contrasting steel, then double-edged and finished with a hand migaki polish that lifts the Damascus grain.

The handle is oil-stained Japanese white oak (kashi) with a checkered grip for wet hands, ringed by a brass guard. It ships in an oil-stained wood sheath bound with a leather band — a working scabbard, not a display case.

One-of-a-kind

Free-forged, not stamped

Each blade is drawn out by hand over fire, so the Damascus pattern and balance are unique to the piece you receive.

Field-proven

Awarded in Europe

Twice given the Grand Prize at a European outdoor knife show, judged over six cutting tests, and inducted into its hall of fame. name & years to confirm

Do-everything

Branch to game to mushroom

Trims branches, carves and handles bushcraft, dresses fish and game, and works mushroom foraging — one edge for the whole field.

Sheathed — oil-stained wood case, leather band

Care & use

  • High-carbon steel: wipe dry after use and keep a light film of oil on the blade to guard against rust.
  • Re-oil the wood handle and sheath occasionally to keep them sealed against moisture.
  • Cut on wood or in the field; avoid bone-heavy chopping and prying that can chip a hard, thin edge.
  • Sharpen on whetstones to preserve the double-edge geometry.

Within the line

A higher-carbon sibling.

The same kennata pattern is also forged in a darker-handled Blue-Super steel variant, supplied with a leather case. The broader line spans roughly US$238–335 depending on steel and finish. The German specialist retailer DICTUM carries Toyokuni blades for the European market.

Tell us how you plan to use the blade and we will confirm the right configuration with the smith before you order.

Ask which configuration suits you — in English

Questions

Before you order.

Can I order or ask questions in English?
Yes. English inquiries are handled for the maker by Watari — you write in English and are answered in English. Pricing, availability, shipping and configuration details are confirmed directly with the smith. details to confirm
Is each blade really unique?
Yes. Because the steel is free-forged by hand rather than machine-stamped, the Damascus pattern, weight and balance vary slightly from piece to piece. The figures listed are for the featured configuration.
What is the steel, and how hard is it?
The featured blade uses an Aogami #2 (Blue-2) high-carbon core forge-welded into 15 Damascus layers. The exact HRC hardness is confirmed with the smith on request. HRC to confirm
What about the awards and overseas availability?
Toyokuni blades have twice taken the Grand Prize at a European outdoor knife show — judged over six cutting tests — and been inducted into its hall of fame. The exact show name and years are confirmed with the maker. Blades are also carried in Europe by DICTUM. show name & years to confirm
How is it priced in my currency?
The featured configuration is US$302 on the maker's English store, within a line of roughly US$238–335. The yen equivalent is approximately ¥44,000. ¥ to confirm
